errors.tooLong: Too long — looks like microseconds. Truncate to 13 digits.
errors.secondsTooLarge: Value too large for seconds. Switch to Milliseconds?

descriptions.whatIs: A Unix timestamp is the number of seconds elapsed since 1970-01-01T00:00:00Z (the Unix epoch). It is timezone-agnostic — the same number always refers to the same instant in time, regardless of where you are.
descriptions.secMs: Most Unix tools and log files use seconds (10 digits today). JavaScript's Date.getTime(), Java's System.currentTimeMillis(), and many web APIs use milliseconds (13 digits today).
descriptions.y2k38: Systems that store Unix seconds in a signed 32-bit integer overflow on 2038-01-19 03:14:07 UTC. Modern 64-bit systems are not affected.
descriptions.tz: A timestamp itself has no timezone. The human-readable form (e.g. 2024-05-01 00:00:00) depends on which timezone you display it in. This tool always shows both Local and UTC.
descriptions.aeoDefinition: Unix Timestamp Converter is a free online tool for converting between Unix timestamps and human-readable dates. Supports seconds and milliseconds with live clock. Runs entirely in your browser.
descriptions.faq1A: A Unix timestamp is the number of seconds (or milliseconds) elapsed since January 1, 1970 UTC (the Unix epoch). It's a universal, timezone-independent way to represent time.
descriptions.faq2A: Yes. The tool shows both UTC and your local timezone. You can convert timestamps to dates in either timezone.
descriptions.faq3Q: What is the Unix timestamp range?
descriptions.faq3A: Standard 32-bit signed integers support timestamps up to January 19, 2038 (the Year 2038 problem). This tool supports a much wider range using JavaScript's Date object.
descriptions.stepsTitle: How to Convert a Unix Timestamp
descriptions.step1Text: Paste a Unix timestamp in the Timestamp→Date panel, or pick a date and time in the Date→Timestamp panel.
descriptions.step2Text: Select the unit (auto-detection works for most cases). Use the Quick buttons for "Now" or "Today".
descriptions.step3Text: The result shows Local time, ISO 8601, RFC 2822, relative time, and timezone info — all at a glance.
